statement: Medusa was a Gorgon whose gaze could turn any living creature to stone instantly, making her one of the most feared monsters in Greek mythology.
quizQuestion: What power did Medusa's gaze have?
explanation: Medusa was one of three Gorgon sisters in Greek mythology. Her gaze was said to instantly petrify anyone who looked directly at her. Perseus slew her by viewing her reflection in a polished shield, avoiding direct eye contact. After her death, Pegasus and Chrysaor sprang from her blood.
wowFactor: Even in death, Medusa's severed head retained its power — Perseus used it as a weapon to turn his enemies to stone.

variants.question: What happened if you looked Medusa in the eyes?
Medusa is the Greek monster whose gaze caused what?
True or false: Medusa could turn people to stone with her gaze.
The Gorgon ___ could petrify anyone who looked at her.

visualDescription: A woman with writhing snakes for hair, green skin glowing with menace, pixel art style with stone grey accents.

statement: Medusa's hair was transformed into writhing serpents by Athena as punishment after Poseidon assaulted Medusa in Athena's temple.
quizQuestion: Who transformed Medusa's hair into snakes?
explanation: In Ovid's retelling, Medusa was once a beautiful priestess. Poseidon violated her in Athena's temple. Furious at the desecration of her sacred space, Athena punished Medusa by transforming her beautiful hair into venomous snakes and making her gaze lethal.
wowFactor: Medusa was actually the victim — Athena punished her for something done to her, making this one of mythology's most morally complex stories.

variants.question: Which goddess cursed Medusa with serpent hair?
Athena punished Medusa by transforming her hair into what?
True or false: Zeus transformed Medusa's hair into snakes.
___ punished Medusa by turning her hair into living snakes.

visualDescription: Athena in golden armor pointing a divine finger at a kneeling Medusa, serpents erupting from her hair, pixel art style.

statement: Perseus slew Medusa by looking only at her reflection in a polished bronze shield, then beheaded her with a sword gifted by Hermes.
quizQuestion: How did Perseus avoid Medusa's deadly gaze?
explanation: Perseus was tasked with killing Medusa by King Polydectes. The gods aided him: Athena gave him a mirrored shield, Hermes provided winged sandals and a sword, and the nymphs gave him a kibisis (satchel) and the helm of Hades for invisibility. By watching Medusa's reflection, he safely approached and beheaded her.
wowFactor: From Medusa's severed neck sprang Pegasus the winged horse and Chrysaor the giant — her offspring with Poseidon, born only in death.

variants.question: What tool let Perseus safely approach Medusa?
Perseus used a polished shield as a mirror to defeat which monster?
True or false: Perseus killed Medusa by looking at her reflection in a shield.
Perseus killed ___ by watching her reflection in a polished shield.

visualDescription: Perseus holding a bronze shield showing Medusa's reflection, sword raised, pixel art with dramatic lighting.

statement: Poseidon was the Greek god of the sea, earthquakes, and horses, wielding a trident as his symbol of power over the oceans.
quizQuestion: What weapon did Poseidon carry?
explanation: Poseidon (Neptune in Roman mythology) was one of the twelve Olympian gods, brother of Zeus and Hades. The three brothers divided the cosmos after defeating the Titans — Zeus took the sky, Hades the underworld, and Poseidon the seas. His trident could shatter rocks, create earthquakes, and summon storms.
wowFactor: Poseidon didn't just rule the sea — he could cause earthquakes by striking the ground with his trident, earning him the title 'Earth-Shaker'.

variants.question: What is Poseidon's iconic weapon and symbol?
The trident is the symbol of which Greek sea god?
True or false: Poseidon was the Greek god of the sea and earthquakes.
___ was the Greek god of the sea who wielded a trident.

visualDescription: Poseidon rising from ocean waves, white beard flowing, gripping a massive three-pronged trident, pixel art with deep blue tones.

statement: Greek mythology credits Poseidon with creating the first horse by striking his trident against a rock, which is why he was also worshipped as a patron of horsemanship.
quizQuestion: Which god created the first horse in Greek myth?
explanation: In Greek mythology, Poseidon created the horse from the sea foam or by striking a rock with his trident. He was deeply associated with horses and was invoked by sailors but also by jockeys and charioteers. The horse's connection to waves — both surge with power and rhythm — is reflected in his dual domain.
wowFactor: The Greeks thought horses looked like crashing waves, so it made perfect sense that the god of the sea also created the horse.

variants.question: In Greek myth, who struck a rock to create the first horse?
Poseidon was the patron deity of what animal besides sea creatures?
True or false: Poseidon was associated with horses in Greek mythology.
___ created the first horse by striking his trident on a rock.

visualDescription: Poseidon slamming trident into stone as a majestic horse leaps out surrounded by sea spray, pixel art with teal and white palette.

statement: Poseidon and Athena competed for patronage of Athens: Poseidon struck the Acropolis with his trident to produce saltwater, but Athena's olive tree gift was judged more useful.
quizQuestion: Who won the contest for Athens' patronage?
explanation: In a divine contest for the city's patronage, both Poseidon and Athena offered gifts. Poseidon struck the Acropolis and produced a saltwater spring (or in some versions a horse). Athena produced an olive tree. The citizens (or the gods acting as judges) chose Athena's gift as more useful — giving food, oil, and wood — and named the city Athens in her honor.
wowFactor: Athens is named after Athena specifically because she beat Poseidon in a divine contest judged by the ability to give the best gift.

variants.question: Which goddess won the contest to name the city Athens?
Athena won Athens by gifting the citizens what plant?
True or false: Poseidon lost the contest for Athens' patronage to Athena.

visualDescription: Athena and Poseidon facing off on the Acropolis, an olive tree growing behind her and a saltwater spring behind him, pixel art.

statement: Freyja is the Norse goddess of love, fertility, beauty, and war, associated with magic, prophecy, and the Valkyries who choose warriors slain in battle.
quizQuestion: Freyja is the Norse goddess of love and what else?
explanation: Freyja (also spelled Freya) is one of the most prominent goddesses in Norse mythology, belonging to the Vanir tribe of gods. She is associated with love, sex, beauty, gold, magic (seiðr), war, and death. As mistress of Fólkvangr, she receives half of those slain in battle, while Odin takes the other half to Valhalla.
wowFactor: Freyja gets first pick of warriors slain in battle — she takes half of all fallen heroes, even before Odin.

variants.question: Which Norse goddess claimed half of all warriors slain in battle?
Freyja ruled what Norse afterlife realm for fallen warriors?
True or false: Freyja is associated with both love and war in Norse mythology.
The Norse goddess ___ was associated with love, magic, and war.

visualDescription: Freyja in golden armor riding a chariot pulled by two large cats, pixel art with amber and gold palette.

statement: Freyja possesses the Brísingamen, a legendary necklace of extraordinary beauty crafted by four dwarf brothers, which she obtained by spending a night with each of them.
quizQuestion: What is the name of Freyja's legendary necklace?
explanation: The Brísingamen (Old Norse for 'necklace of the Brísings') is described as an exceptionally beautiful piece of jewelry crafted by four dwarf brothers called the Brísings. In the Sörla þáttr, Freyja desired it so greatly she agreed to spend one night with each dwarf. Loki later stole it and Heimdall had to retrieve it after a battle with Loki.
wowFactor: The Brísingamen was so coveted that Loki disguised himself as a fly to steal it — and Heimdall shapeshifted into a seal to fight him for it.

variants.question: Which legendary jewel did Freyja obtain from four dwarfs?
Brísingamen is a legendary necklace belonging to which Norse goddess?
True or false: Freyja's necklace Brísingamen was crafted by dwarf brothers.
Freyja's prized necklace ___ was made by four dwarfs.

sensitivityNote: Mythological context involving transactions of intimacy
visualDescription: Freyja fastening a glowing golden necklace while four stout dwarfs look on admiringly, pixel art with warm amber tones.

statement: Freyja travels across the sky in a chariot pulled by two large grey cats, and she is also known to ride a boar named Hildisvíni into battle.
quizQuestion: What animals pull Freyja's chariot?
explanation: In Norse mythology, Freyja's chariot is pulled by two large cats. The cats are unnamed in most sources but are sometimes described as grey or blue. Because of this association, cats were considered sacred to Freyja. Viking-era brides prayed to Freyja, and cats were given as wedding gifts. She also owns a boar named Hildisvíni ('battle swine') and a cloak of falcon feathers.
wowFactor: Vikings kept cats partly because cats were Freyja's sacred animals — the goddess of love and war had the ultimate power pets.

variants.question: What sacred animals pulled Freyja's divine chariot?
Freyja owned a boar named Hildisvíni and a chariot pulled by what?
True or false: Freyja's chariot is pulled by large cats.
Freyja's chariot was pulled by two large ___.

visualDescription: Two enormous grey cats pulling a Norse chariot through stormy skies, Freyja standing proudly behind them, pixel art style.

statement: The Minotaur was a creature with the body of a man and the head of a bull, imprisoned in the Labyrinth of Crete built by the master craftsman Daedalus.
quizQuestion: Where was the Minotaur imprisoned?
explanation: The Minotaur (Greek: Minōtauros — 'Bull of Minos') was the offspring of Queen Pasiphae of Crete and a divine bull. King Minos had Daedalus build the Labyrinth — an elaborate maze — to contain the creature. Athens was forced to send tributes of seven young men and seven maidens periodically as food for the beast.
wowFactor: The Labyrinth was so well designed that even Daedalus himself could barely find his way out after building it.

variants.question: What structure held the Minotaur captive in Crete?
The Labyrinth was built by Daedalus to imprison which monster?
True or false: The Minotaur was imprisoned in a maze called the Labyrinth.
Daedalus built the ___ to contain the half-man, half-bull Minotaur.

visualDescription: A massive bull-headed humanoid standing in a stone maze, torchlight flickering on the labyrinth walls, pixel art with dark shadows.

statement: Theseus slew the Minotaur and found his way out of the Labyrinth using a thread given to him by the princess Ariadne, who had fallen in love with him.
quizQuestion: How did Theseus navigate out of the Labyrinth?
explanation: Theseus, prince of Athens, volunteered to enter the Labyrinth as one of the tributes. Princess Ariadne, daughter of King Minos, fell in love with him and gave him a ball of thread (the 'clew of Ariadne'). He tied one end at the entrance, slew the Minotaur with his bare hands (or a sword), then followed the thread back out. The English word 'clue' derives from this story.
wowFactor: The word 'clue' literally comes from Ariadne's thread — a 'clew' was an old word for a ball of yarn, and finding your way out became finding a clue.

variants.question: What did Ariadne give Theseus to escape the Labyrinth?
Theseus used Ariadne's thread to navigate which maze?
True or false: Theseus used a thread from Ariadne to find his way out of the Labyrinth.
Theseus used ___ thread to find his way back through the Labyrinth.

visualDescription: Theseus battling the Minotaur with a glowing thread trailing behind him through dark stone corridors, pixel art action pose.

statement: Athens was forced to send seven young men and seven maidens to Crete every nine years as tribute to feed the Minotaur, as punishment for killing King Minos's son.
quizQuestion: How many tributes did Athens send to the Minotaur?
explanation: The tribute of Athens to Crete was fourteen young people — seven boys and seven girls — sent every seven (or nine, depending on the source) years. King Minos had imposed this penalty after the Athenians killed his son Androgeos. The tributes were sent into the Labyrinth as food for the Minotaur. Theseus volunteered to be one of the tributes specifically to end this arrangement.
wowFactor: Theseus convinced his father he'd sail back with white sails if he survived — but forgot, and his father jumped to his death seeing the black sails of mourning.

variants.question: What tribute did Athens send periodically to feed the Minotaur?
Athens paid tribute to Crete because Athenians killed whose son?
True or false: Athens sent fourteen youths as tribute to feed the Minotaur.
Athens sent seven men and seven ___ as tribute to Crete every nine years.

visualDescription: A group of fourteen young Athenians chained together boarding a black-sailed ship heading toward Crete, pixel art with dark tones.

statement: Ganesha, the Hindu elephant-headed god, is widely revered as the remover of obstacles and the patron deity of new beginnings, arts, and wisdom.
quizQuestion: What is Ganesha known as the remover of?
explanation: Ganesha (also spelled Ganesh or Ganesa) is one of the best-known and most worshipped deities in Hinduism. He is the son of Shiva and Parvati. His elephant head represents wisdom, understanding, and a discriminating intellect. He is invoked at the beginning of any major undertaking — prayers, weddings, business ventures — to clear the path of obstacles.
wowFactor: Before beginning anything important — a business, a wedding, a school exam — Hindus pray to Ganesha first. He has been doing this job for over 2,000 years.

variants.question: Which Hindu god is prayed to at the start of new endeavors?
Ganesha's elephant head symbolizes what quality?
True or false: Ganesha is known as the Hindu remover of obstacles.
In Hinduism, ___ is invoked to remove obstacles before new endeavors.

visualDescription: Ganesha seated with elephant head, multiple arms holding sacred objects, surrounded by lotus flowers, pixel art in warm saffron tones.

statement: Ganesha is depicted with only one complete tusk — the other is broken, and according to one myth he broke it himself to use as a stylus to write the Mahabharata.
quizQuestion: Why does Ganesha have one broken tusk?
acceptableAnswers: He wrote the Mahabharata with it
explanation: One popular myth states that Ganesha agreed to transcribe the Mahabharata as dictated by the sage Vyasa, but his stylus broke. Rather than interrupt the dictation, he snapped off his own tusk and used it as a pen. This story symbolizes the importance of knowledge and the sacrifice made in its pursuit. Another version says Parashurama threw his axe at Ganesha and he didn't dodge out of respect.
wowFactor: Ganesha snapped off his own tusk rather than pause the world's longest epic poem — 100,000 verses, written without stopping.

variants.question: What did Ganesha use his broken tusk to write?
Ganesha broke his tusk to use it as a stylus for what epic text?
True or false: Ganesha broke his own tusk to write the Mahabharata.
Ganesha broke his tusk to use as a pen when writing the ___.

visualDescription: Ganesha writing on a palm leaf with his broken tusk, a scroll of the Mahabharata unfurling endlessly beside him, pixel art.

statement: Ganesha's vahana (divine vehicle or mount) is a mouse named Mushika, symbolizing the ability to penetrate into hidden places and overcome even the smallest obstacles.
quizQuestion: What is Ganesha's divine mount?
explanation: In Hindu iconography, each deity has a vahana — a divine vehicle or mount. Ganesha's vahana is Mushika, a mouse. Despite Ganesha's large size, he rides a tiny mouse, which represents mastering the ego (the mouse represents desire and the mind's tendency to gnaw at things). Mushika can penetrate the smallest spaces, representing that with Ganesha's blessings, one can overcome even minute obstacles.
wowFactor: The massive elephant-headed god riding a tiny mouse isn't ironic — it's deliberate. The mouse represents the ego, and Ganesha rides it to show he is its master.

variants.question: What animal serves as Ganesha's divine vehicle in Hindu tradition?
Mushika is the mouse that serves as the vahana of which Hindu god?
True or false: Ganesha's divine mount is a mouse named Mushika.
Ganesha's vahana, or divine mount, is a small ___.

visualDescription: Enormous Ganesha perched atop a tiny mouse in regal dignity, flowers scattered around them, pixel art with golden hues.

statement: Anubis was the ancient Egyptian jackal-headed god of the dead who presided over mummification and guided souls through the afterlife.
quizQuestion: What animal head does Anubis have?
explanation: Anubis (ancient Egyptian: Inpw) is one of the oldest and most iconic deities in the Egyptian pantheon. He is depicted with the head of a black jackal and the body of a man. Jackals were associated with death because they were scavengers often seen near cemeteries. Anubis was the patron of embalming, protector of tombs, and guide of souls to the Hall of Two Truths for judgment.
wowFactor: Ancient Egyptians chose the jackal as Anubis's form specifically because jackals roamed cemeteries — they transformed a threatening scavenger into a divine guardian.

variants.question: Which Egyptian god is depicted with a black jackal head?
Anubis was the Egyptian god of what domain?
True or false: Anubis has the head of a jackal in Egyptian mythology.
The Egyptian god ___ had a jackal head and oversaw the dead.

visualDescription: Anubis standing tall with black jackal head, holding a golden staff and ankh, Egyptian tomb wall behind him, pixel art.

statement: Anubis weighed the hearts of the deceased against a feather of Ma'at — those whose hearts were heavier than the feather were devoured by the monster Ammit.
quizQuestion: What did Anubis weigh hearts against in the afterlife?
explanation: In the Hall of Two Truths, Anubis placed the heart of the deceased on one side of a great scale and the Feather of Ma'at (representing truth and justice) on the other. If the heart was lighter than or equal to the feather, the soul was deemed righteous and passed to Aaru (the Field of Reeds). If heavier (weighed down by sin), the heart was immediately devoured by Ammit — a hybrid monster — and the soul ceased to exist.
wowFactor: Failing the Egyptian afterlife judgment wasn't hell — it was total annihilation. Your soul was eaten and you simply stopped existing forever.

variants.question: What did Anubis use to judge the weight of a soul's heart?
If a heart outweighed Ma'at's feather, it was eaten by which monster?
True or false: Anubis weighed hearts against a feather to judge the dead.
Anubis weighed the heart against the feather of ___ to judge souls.

visualDescription: Anubis tending a golden scale with a glowing heart on one side and a white feather on the other, hieroglyphs on the walls, pixel art.

statement: Anubis was the patron god of mummification and embalmers — Egyptian morticians wore jackal masks to represent him while performing the sacred mummification rituals.
quizQuestion: Who was the Egyptian patron god of mummification?
explanation: As the god of mummification, Anubis was deeply integrated into Egyptian burial practices. Embalmers wore masks shaped like Anubis's jackal head during the 70-day mummification process to invoke his guidance. He was credited with teaching the Egyptians the art of embalming, reputedly first practiced on Osiris's body. His black color represented both death and the fertile Nile soil — renewal through decay.
wowFactor: Egyptian embalmers literally dressed as Anubis during mummification — wearing jackal masks for 70 days to perform the sacred rites in his name.

variants.question: Which god did Egyptian embalmers invoke by wearing jackal masks?
Anubis was the patron deity of which funerary practice?
True or false: Egyptian embalmers wore jackal masks representing Anubis.
Egyptian embalmers wore ___ masks to channel the god of mummification.

visualDescription: An Egyptian embalmer wearing a jackal mask working over a wrapped mummy, canopic jars lined up nearby, pixel art dim lighting.

statement: Hades was the Greek god and ruler of the underworld, a realm he received when Zeus, Poseidon, and Hades drew lots to divide the cosmos after defeating the Titans.
quizQuestion: How did Hades receive the underworld?
explanation: After the Olympians defeated the Titans, Zeus, Poseidon, and Hades drew lots to divide the world. Zeus drew the sky, Poseidon the sea, and Hades drew the underworld. Although Hades rules the realm of the dead, he is not a death god in the sense of causing death — Thanatos handled death itself. Hades rarely left his realm and was considered stern but fair.
wowFactor: Hades didn't choose his gloomy kingdom — it was literally random chance. He drew the short lot and ended up ruling the dead for eternity.

variants.question: What method determined that Hades would rule the underworld?
Hades, Poseidon, and Zeus divided the cosmos — Hades got what domain?
True or false: Hades received the underworld by drawing lots with his brothers.
Hades received the underworld after drawing ___ with Zeus and Poseidon.

visualDescription: Hades on an obsidian throne in a cavernous hall, Cerberus at his feet, the River Styx glowing behind him, pixel art dark palette.

statement: Hades abducted Persephone and made her queen of the underworld — her mother Demeter's grief caused crops to wither, explaining the origin of winter.
quizQuestion: Who became queen of Hades's underworld?
explanation: Hades abducted Persephone (daughter of Demeter, goddess of the harvest) and made her his wife and queen. Demeter's grief caused all plants to die. Zeus negotiated: Persephone would return, but having eaten pomegranate seeds in the underworld, she was bound to return there for part of each year. This explains the seasons — when Persephone is below, Demeter grieves and it is winter; when she returns, spring blooms.
wowFactor: The ancient Greeks explained all of winter as Demeter's depression — every year, she mourns her daughter's descent into the underworld.

variants.question: Who served as queen alongside Hades in the Greek underworld?
Persephone's absence in the underworld caused her mother to create what season?
True or false: Persephone is the queen of Hades's underworld in Greek myth.
___ was abducted by Hades and became queen of the underworld.

visualDescription: Persephone in a half-dark, half-flowered gown sitting beside Hades on twin thrones, pomegranate seeds on a plate between them, pixel art.

statement: Hades owned a magical helmet of darkness (the Helm of Hades or Cap of Invisibility) that rendered the wearer completely invisible — a weapon used even by the goddess Athena.
quizQuestion: What power did the Helm of Hades grant?
explanation: The Helm of Hades (also called the Cap of Invisibility or Κυνέη) was a magical cap that granted complete invisibility. It was crafted by the Cyclopes during the Titans' War when they also made Zeus's thunderbolt and Poseidon's trident. Hades lent it to Perseus to help slay Medusa, and Athena wore it during the Trojan War to move unseen on the battlefield.
wowFactor: Even Athena borrowed Hades's invisibility helmet during the Trojan War — the ultimate divine stealth weapon, predating modern invisibility cloaks by 3,000 years.

variants.question: What ability did the legendary Helm of Hades confer?
The Cap of Invisibility was a magical item belonging to which Greek god?
True or false: The Helm of Hades granted the power of invisibility.
The Helm of ___ was a cap that made the wearer invisible.

visualDescription: A dark helm fading into shadow while a ghostly outline shows someone still wearing it, pixel art with translucent purple tones.

statement: Fenrir is a monstrous wolf in Norse mythology, son of Loki, prophesied to break free at Ragnarök and swallow Odin whole during the final battle.
quizQuestion: What does Fenrir swallow at Ragnarök?
explanation: Fenrir (Old Norse: Fenrisúlfr) is one of Loki's three monstrous children, along with the Midgard Serpent (Jörmungandr) and Hel. The gods feared his growing power and bound him with a magical ribbon called Gleipnir, made by the dwarfs from impossible things (like the sound of a cat's footstep and the roots of a mountain). At Ragnarök, he breaks free and devours Odin, but is then slain by Odin's son Víðarr.
wowFactor: Fenrir was so terrifying the gods needed magic dwarfs to make him a chain from impossible things — 'the sound of a cat's footstep' and 'the roots of mountains'.

variants.question: Which Norse god does Fenrir devour at Ragnarök?
Fenrir is the monstrous wolf son of which trickster god?
True or false: Fenrir is prophesied to swallow Odin at Ragnarök.
The monstrous wolf ___ is bound until Ragnarök in Norse mythology.

visualDescription: An enormous wolf straining against a thin gleaming ribbon chain, surrounded by Norse runes, pixel art with stormy grey tones.

statement: The gods bound Fenrir with a magical fetter called Gleipnir, crafted by dwarf smiths from six impossible things including the sound of a cat's footstep and the roots of a mountain.
quizQuestion: What is the magical chain binding Fenrir called?
explanation: After Fenrir broke two iron chains (Leyding and Dromi), the gods asked the dwarfs of Svartalfheim to craft an unbreakable fetter. Gleipnir was made from six impossible ingredients: the sound of a cat walking, the beard of a woman, the roots of a mountain, the sinews of a bear, the breath of a fish, and the spittle of a bird. It looked like a thin silk ribbon yet was impossible to break. Tyr sacrificed his hand in Fenrir's mouth to convince the wolf to allow the binding.
wowFactor: Gleipnir is thin as a ribbon yet unbreakable because it's woven from things that don't exist — six impossible materials that the dwarfs harvested from the world.

variants.question: What is the name of the unbreakable fetter that binds Fenrir?
Gleipnir was made from six impossible ingredients to bind which monster?
True or false: Gleipnir is the magical ribbon that binds Fenrir the wolf.
The gods bound Fenrir with ___, a fetter made from impossible things.

visualDescription: A silken-looking ribbon glowing with runes around a massive wolf's neck, dwarfs in the background smithing impossible things, pixel art.

statement: To convince Fenrir to allow the binding, the god Tyr volunteered to place his hand in the wolf's mouth as a pledge of good faith — Fenrir bit it off when he realized he could not break free.
quizQuestion: Which god lost his hand to Fenrir?
explanation: Tyr (Old Norse: Týr) was the Norse god of war, justice, and law. When Fenrir refused to be bound unless a god placed a hand in his mouth as surety, only Tyr was brave enough to do so. When the wolf found he could not escape Gleipnir and realized the gods had broken their oath, he bit off Tyr's hand at the wrist. Tyr's sacrifice is seen as a symbol of law and order requiring personal sacrifice to maintain.
wowFactor: Tyr knowingly sacrificed his hand — he placed it in a wolf's mouth knowing the wolf would bite it off, because maintaining order was worth the price.

variants.question: Which Norse god bravely placed his hand in Fenrir's jaws as pledge?
Tyr lost his hand to Fenrir as the price of binding the wolf with what?
True or false: The god Tyr lost his hand when Fenrir bit it off after the binding.
The Norse god ___ sacrificed his hand as a pledge when Fenrir was bound.

visualDescription: Tyr steadily placing his hand into an enormous wolf's open jaws while the other gods watch in awe, pixel art with muted heroic tones.

statement: Brahma is the Hindu god of creation and the first member of the Trimurti (trinity), depicted with four heads facing the four directions and four arms.
quizQuestion: How many heads does Brahma have?
explanation: Brahma (not to be confused with Brahman, the absolute) is the Hindu god of creation. He is part of the Trimurti: Brahma (creation), Vishnu (preservation), and Shiva (destruction). His four heads are said to have arisen from his wish to observe his creation from all directions. Each head recites one of the four Vedas. Despite being the creator, Brahma is rarely worshipped today — there is only one major temple dedicated to him at Pushkar, India.
wowFactor: Brahma created the universe but is barely worshipped today — he lost favor with Shiva in a mythological dispute and now has only ONE major temple in all of India.

variants.question: How many heads does the Hindu creator god Brahma have?
Brahma's four heads each recite one of which sacred Hindu texts?
True or false: Brahma is the Hindu god of creation with four heads.
In the Hindu Trimurti, ___ is the god of creation.

visualDescription: Brahma seated on a lotus with four bearded heads looking in four directions, each hand holding sacred objects, pixel art gold palette.

statement: Brahma's consort is Saraswati, the goddess of knowledge, music, and arts, and his vahana (divine mount) is a white swan or goose called Hamsa.
quizQuestion: What is Brahma's divine mount?
explanation: Brahma's divine mount (vahana) is Hamsa, a white swan or goose. In Hindu symbolism, the swan represents wisdom, discrimination, and the ability to separate the real from the unreal — it was believed swans could separate milk from water when mixed together. This symbolism connects Brahma to pure wisdom and discernment. His consort Saraswati also rides a swan.
wowFactor: The Hindu swan Hamsa represents the ability to separate milk from water — symbolizing wisdom's ability to sort truth from illusion, which is why the creator god rides it.

variants.question: Which divine animal is Brahma's vahana in Hindu tradition?
Hamsa, the divine swan, is the vahana of which Hindu creator god?
True or false: Brahma's divine mount is a white swan called Hamsa.
Brahma rides a divine white ___ called Hamsa as his vahana.

visualDescription: Brahma with his four heads seated on a large white swan floating on a lotus-filled lake, pixel art in soft white and golden tones.

statement: Brahma originally had five heads, but Shiva cut off the fifth after Brahma spoke disrespectfully to him — this is why Brahma now has only four heads.
quizQuestion: Who cut off Brahma's fifth head?
explanation: According to the Shiva Purana, Brahma originally had five heads. After Brahma claimed to be superior to Vishnu, Shiva appeared and Brahma's fifth head insulted Shiva. In response, Shiva used his fingernail (or a manifestation of Bhairava) to cut off the fifth head. Because Shiva severed a Brahmin's head (Brahma being the supreme Brahmin), he had to carry the skull as penance — the origin of Shiva's 'Kapala' (skull cup) iconography.
wowFactor: Shiva cut off Brahma's arrogant fifth head and then had to carry the skull as punishment for killing a Brahmin — even gods face karmic consequences.

variants.question: Which god severed Brahma's fifth head in Hindu mythology?
Shiva cut off Brahma's fifth head as punishment for what act?

statement: Set (also Seth) was the Egyptian god of chaos, storms, and the desert — he murdered his brother Osiris and scattered the body to prevent his resurrection.
quizQuestion: What did Set do to his brother Osiris?
acceptableAnswers: Killed and cut up his body
explanation: Set was one of the major Egyptian deities associated with chaos, storms, the desert, violence, and foreigners. In the Osiris myth, Set murdered Osiris — in some versions he lured Osiris into a coffin, sealed it, and threw it in the Nile; in others he dismembered the body into 14 (or 42) pieces scattered across Egypt. Isis gathered the pieces and, with Anubis's help, reassembled and mummified Osiris, who then became king of the dead.
wowFactor: Set cut Osiris into 14 pieces and scattered them across Egypt — Isis found every piece except one, and the world's first mummy was made from what remained.

variants.question: Set murdered which Egyptian god and scattered his dismembered body?
The Egyptian god Set was associated with chaos and what harsh landscape?
True or false: Set murdered his brother Osiris in Egyptian mythology.
___ was the Egyptian god of chaos who murdered his brother Osiris.

sensitivityNote: References murder and dismemberment in mythological context
visualDescription: Set with his strange animal head (long snout, squared ears) standing over a fallen Osiris in a desert scene, pixel art dark tones.

statement: Set battled Horus (Osiris's son) for eighty years for rule of Egypt — Set tore out Horus's eye and Horus tore off Set's testicles before the gods declared Horus the rightful king.
quizQuestion: How long did Set and Horus battle for Egypt?
explanation: The Contendings of Horus and Set is an Egyptian mythological text describing an 80-year divine tribunal and battle for the throne of Egypt. Set, as Osiris's brother, and Horus, as Osiris's son, both claimed the right to rule. The conflict involved trials, battles, and deliberation by the gods. Ultimately the gods ruled in favor of Horus. The Eye of Horus — a powerful symbol of protection — originated from the eye Set tore out during combat.
wowFactor: The Eye of Horus, still used as a symbol of protection and good luck today, commemorates the eye torn out during an 80-year divine war for Egypt's throne.

variants.question: For how many years did Horus and Set battle for the Egyptian throne?

Horus battled ___ for eighty years before winning Egypt's throne.

visualDescription: Horus with falcon head and Set with his strange beast head locked in combat, the Eye of Horus glowing in the background, pixel art.

statement: Set is depicted with the head of an unidentified animal with a long curved snout and tall squared ears — called the 'Set animal' — no living species has ever been identified as the creature.
quizQuestion: What makes Set's animal form unique among Egyptian gods?
distractors: It's based on a mythical creature
It has the head of a crocodile
acceptableAnswers: No one knows what animal it is
explanation: Unlike other Egyptian gods whose animals are clearly identifiable (falcon for Horus, ibis for Thoth, jackal for Anubis), Set's head is a mystery. The 'Set animal' has a long, slightly curved snout, tall square-topped ears, and a forked tail. Egyptologists have proposed it could be an aardvark, a pig, a donkey, an okapi, or even a chimera. No consensus has been reached — it may represent a creature that has gone extinct or was purely symbolic.
wowFactor: After thousands of years of study, Egyptologists still cannot identify what real animal Set's head depicts — the god of chaos has a head from an unknown beast.
